articles.title: Tragedy in Wolverhampton: Two Children Die in House Fire
articles.summary: On Friday evening, a devastating house fire broke out in Wolverhampton, claiming the lives of two young children. Emergency services, including West Midlands Police, Fire Service, and Ambulance Service, responded to the scene on Mason Street around 8:30 PM. Firefighters bravely entered the burning property and rescued the children, but despite immediate advanced life support, both were pronounced dead at the scene. Two other children and a woman had already escaped the house before emergency crews arrived; they were assessed by medics but did not require hospital treatment. The fire caused extensive damage to the property, and a cordon has been established as police investigate the cause of the blaze. A spokesperson expressed condolences to the family and all affected by this heartbreaking incident. The community is in mourning as authorities work to determine the circumstances leading to this tragedy.

articles.title: Drama Erupts in I'm a Celebrity Final
articles.summary: The live final of I'm a Celebrity... Get Me Out of Here! South Africa was marked by a heated argument among the all-star cast. The conflict began when actor Adam Thomas launched a tirade at footballer Jimmy Bullard after Bullard forfeited a challenge, nearly causing Thomas's elimination. Hosts Ant and Dec had to raise their voices to control the row, which also saw singer Sinitta and TV personality Gemma Collins walk off set. Despite the drama, Thomas was crowned the winner. The incident sparked discussions about behavior and editing. Producers did not broadcast Thomas's full outburst, which other contestants described as 'unbroadcastable' and 'deeply traumatic'. Craig Charles revealed that Thomas was so aggressive it looked like he might attack Bullard. Bullard called the reaction 'pathetic' and explained he quit because he wanted to go home. Thomas later posted on Instagram, saying he stayed true to himself and was proud of his time in the jungle. The show is a spin-off featuring past contestants, and Thomas had previously finished third in 2016. The series also included other finalists like Sir Mo Farah, David Haye, Craig Charles, and Harry Redknapp.

articles.title: US-Spain Tensions Threaten NATO Unity
articles.summary: A leaked Pentagon email has revealed US plans to punish Spain for not supporting the US-Israel military campaign against Iran. The email suggested measures including seeking Spain's suspension from NATO, though no treaty provision allows expulsion. European leaders at a summit in Cyprus strongly defended Spain. Dutch Prime Minister Rob Jetten stated Spain remains a full NATO member, and a German official saw no reason for change. Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni criticized the tensions, noting that public opinion in Europe is turning against US President Donald Trump. Meloni herself has denied US use of an Italian airbase for operations against Iran. The email also hinted at reviewing US support for the UK's claim to the Falkland Islands due to UK reluctance to fully back the war. Trump is particularly angry with Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez, though Sanchez insists Spain is meeting its NATO obligations. The incident highlights growing rifts between the US and its European allies over the Iran conflict.

articles.title: Everest Flood Warning System Fails Due to Neglect
articles.summary: An early flood warning system in the Everest region of Nepal, which was designed to protect thousands of people from glacial lake outburst floods, has been allowed to fall into disrepair. The system, installed after the dangerous Imja glacial lake was partially drained in 2016, has not been maintained or inspected for years. Villagers report that siren towers are rusting, batteries have been stolen, and satellite data transmission for mobile phone alerts is unreliable. The Imja lake, located at over 5,000 meters above sea level, was reduced in depth by 3.5 meters as part of a $3.5 million risk reduction project. However, scientists warn that global warming is accelerating glacier melt in the Hindu Kush Himalaya region, causing many glacial lakes to expand and increasing the risk of catastrophic floods. In the Everest region alone, there have been five glacial lake floods in the last five decades. Local leaders and NGOs have repeatedly requested the Department of Hydrology and Meteorology to repair the system, but their appeals have been ignored. The failure of the warning system puts not only villagers but also trekkers, tourists, and climbers at risk, especially during the peak spring season.

articles.title: DOJ Drops Investigation of Fed Chair; Court Blocks Asylum Ban
articles.summary: The Justice Department has ended its criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, who was being probed for allegedly lying to Congress about cost overruns at the Fed's headquarters. Instead, the Fed's inspector general will review the matter. This decision clears the way for Kevin Warsh, President Trump's nominee to replace Powell, to be confirmed. Republican Senator Thom Tillis had threatened to block Warsh's nomination unless the DOJ dropped the probe. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt urged Tillis to confirm Warsh quickly, stating that holding up the nomination is hurting the economy. Meanwhile, a federal appeals court ruled that President Trump's executive order banning asylum claims at the U.S. southern border is illegal. The three-judge panel stated that immigration law grants individuals the right to apply for asylum, and the president cannot override that process. The administration may appeal to the Supreme Court. In other news, the FDA announced it will fast-track reviews of three psychedelic drugs for treating mental health conditions. These developments highlight ongoing tensions between the executive branch, the judiciary, and independent agencies like the Fed.

articles.title: Soldier Indicted for Insider Trading on Prediction Market After Maduro Raid
articles.summary: A U.S. Special Forces soldier who helped plan the January capture of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro has been indicted for allegedly using classified information about the raid to place bets on the prediction market Polymarket. The soldier, stationed at Fort Bragg, North Carolina, won over $400,000 through a series of bets on Maduro's removal. The indictment raises concerns about insider trading and the need for regulation of prediction markets. These markets allow users to bet on the outcome of events, and the case highlights how sensitive information can be exploited for financial gain. Experts argue that clearer rules are needed to prevent such abuse, as prediction markets grow in popularity. The soldier's arrest is a landmark case that could shape future oversight of these platforms.
